title: Right view of tree
description: Print the nodes visible when the binary tree is viewed from the right side

examples.explanation: Step 1: Initialize result=[], queue=[1]. Level 0: size=1, process node 1 (last in level), add 1 to result. Add children: queue becomes [2,3]. Step 2: Level 1: size=2. Process node 2 (not last), add child 5: queue=[3,5]. Process node 3 (last in level), add 3 to result. Add child 4: queue=[5,4]. Step 3: Level 2: size=2. Process node 5 (not last), no children. Process node 4 (last in level), add 4 to result. Queue empty. Final result=[1,3,4].

Step 1: Initialize result=[], queue=[1]. Level 0: size=1, process node 1 (last in level), add 1 to result. Add right child 3: queue=[3]. Step 2: Level 1: size=1, process node 3 (last in level), add 3 to result. Queue empty. Final result=[1,3].

approaches.name: Approach 1: Level Order Traversal with Queue
approaches.summary: Perform a breadth-first search (BFS) using a queue to traverse the tree level by level, and for each level, record the last node encountered. This captures the rightmost node at each depth, which is visible from the right side.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(w) where w is the maximum width of the tree (typically O(n) in worst-case for a skewed tree)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Right View of Binary Tree using Level Order Traversal
ladders.desc: Complete solution to print rightmost nodes at each level using BFS with queue
ladders.explanation: The right view of a binary tree consists of nodes visible when viewing from the right side. At each depth level, only the rightmost node is visible. This problem can be solved efficiently using level order traversal (BFS) with a queue. The intuition is simple: during BFS, we process nodes level by level, and the last node encountered at each level is the rightmost node for that level. We perform standard BFS using a queue, but with a key modification - we track when we've reached the end of each level. For each level, we record the last node's value before moving to the next level. This approach works because BFS naturally processes nodes left-to-right within each level, so the final node in each level's processing is guaranteed to be the rightmost. The algorithm: 1) Initialize empty result list and queue with root. 2) While queue not empty: a) Get current level size. b) Process all nodes at current level: i) Pop node from queue. ii) If this is last node in level, add to result. iii) Add left then right children to queue if they exist. 3) Return result. Time complexity is O(n) where n is number of nodes since we visit each node once. Space complexity is O(w) where w is maximum width (number of nodes at widest level), which is O(n) in worst-case for complete trees. Common mistakes: forgetting to handle null root, not tracking level boundaries correctly, adding nodes in wrong order (should be left then right for proper BFS). The connection to L1 is that this uses the level boundary tracking technique as its core mechanism.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize result=[], queue=[1]. Level 0: size=1, process node 1 (last in level), add 1 to result. Add children: queue becomes [2,3]. Step 2: Level 1: size=2. Process node 2 (not last), add child 5: queue=[3,5]. Process node 3 (last in level), add 3 to result. Add child 4: queue=[5,4]. Step 3: Level 2: size=2. Process node 5 (not last), no children. Process node 4 (last in level), add 4 to result. Queue empty. Final result=[1,3,4].

Step 1: Initialize result=[], queue=[1]. Level 0: size=1, process node 1 (last in level), add 1 to result. Add right child 3: queue=[3]. Step 2: Level 1: size=1, process node 3 (last in level), add 3 to result. Queue empty. Final result=[1,3].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Tracking Level Boundaries in BFS
ladders.desc: Identifying when we've completed processing all nodes at current level
ladders.explanation: The core challenge in implementing right view with BFS is knowing when we've reached the last node in each level. This sub-routine solves exactly that problem. The intuition: during standard BFS, nodes from different levels get mixed in the queue. We need to process nodes level-by-level to identify the rightmost node. The technique: before processing each level, record the current queue size (which equals number of nodes at that level). Then process exactly that many nodes. The last node processed will be the rightmost node for that level. Algorithm: 1) Get level_size = len(queue). 2) For i in range(level_size): a) node = queue.pop(0). b) Process node. c) If i == level_size-1, this is last node in level. d) Add children to queue. 3) Repeat until queue empty. Time complexity for this sub-problem is O(n) as we process each node once. Space complexity is O(w) for queue storage. Common mistakes: not updating queue size correctly, using while loop without tracking level size, confusing when to check for last node. This feeds into L0 by providing the mechanism to identify level boundaries, which is essential for capturing rightmost nodes. Real pattern: level_size = len(queue); for i in range(level_size): node = queue.pop(0); if i == level_size-1: result.append(node.val); if node.left: queue.append(node.left); if node.right: queue.append(node.right).

examples.input: Queue with level 0: [A], level 1: [B,C] after processing
examples.output: Level boundaries at: after processing 1 node (A), after processing 2 nodes (B,C)
examples.trace: Initial: queue=[A]. Step 1: level_size=1. Process i=0: node=A, i==0 (last since level_size-1=0). Queue becomes []. Add A's children B,C: queue=[B,C]. Step 2: level_size=2. Process i=0: node=B, i!=1 (not last). Queue=[C]. Add B's children: none. Process i=1: node=C, i==1 (last). Queue=[]. Add C's children: none. Boundaries identified after processing 1 node then 2 nodes.

Initial: queue=[1,2,3,4]. Step 1: level_size=4? WRONG - this assumes all nodes at same level. Correct: First get actual level sizes. Actually queue should only contain current level nodes. Let's trace correctly: Start with queue=[1]. level_size=1. Process i=0: node=1 (last). Add children 2,3: queue=[2,3]. Now level_size=2. Process i=0: node=2 (not last). Add child 4: queue=[3,4]. Process i=1: node=3 (last). Add children: none. Queue=[4]. level_size=1. Process i=0: node=4 (last). Boundaries: after 1 node, after 2 nodes, after 1 node.

testCases.input: Level with 3 nodes each having 2 children

ladders.title: Core Logic — Last Node Identification in Level Order
ladders.desc: The insight that rightmost node equals last node processed at each level
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight that makes the BFS approach work for right view problem. The intuition: when performing level order traversal (BFS), nodes are processed left-to-right within each level. Therefore, the last node visited at each level must be the rightmost node at that depth. This works because: 1) BFS uses a queue (FIFO structure). 2) We enqueue children left-first, right-second. 3) Within a level, nodes are dequeued in the order they were enqueued. 4) Since right child is enqueued after left child at each parent, the rightmost node at each level will be the last one dequeued for that level. The algorithm: perform standard BFS but record the node value whenever we're processing the final node in current level. We determine 'final node' by tracking how many nodes belong to current level (using the level boundary technique from L1). Time complexity for this logic is O(1) per node since we just check if current node is last in level. Space complexity is O(1) extra beyond BFS requirements. Common mistakes: confusing rightmost with deepest, not considering that right view shows only one node per level, trying to compare node values instead of positions. This feeds into L1 by providing the purpose for tracking level boundaries - we need to know when we've reached the last node. Real pattern: if i == level_size-1: result.append(node.val).

examples.trace: Nodes in level from left to right: A, B, C. BFS processes them in this order. When processing: A (not last), B (not last), C (last in level). Therefore C is identified as rightmost node. This works because C is physically rightmost in tree at this level.
Only one node in level. level_size=1. Process i=0: since level_size-1=0, i==0 is true. Therefore X is identified as rightmost (and only) node at this level.

ladders.title: Building Block — Level Order Traversal (BFS) on Binary Tree
ladders.desc: Traversing tree level by level using queue
ladders.explanation: Level order traversal, also known as Breadth-First Search (BFS), visits nodes level by level from top to bottom, left to right. The intuition: instead of going deep down one branch (like DFS), we explore all nodes at current depth before moving deeper. This is achieved using a queue data structure. Algorithm: 1) Initialize queue with root node. 2) While queue not empty: a) Dequeue front node. b) Process node. c) Enqueue left child then right child if they exist. This ensures nodes at same level are processed before their children, and within a level, left nodes before right nodes. Time complexity is O(n) where n is number of nodes. Space complexity is O(w) where w is maximum width (nodes at widest level). For a complete binary tree, worst-case space is O(n). Common mistakes: forgetting to check if children exist before enqueuing, using stack instead of queue (gives DFS), not handling null root. This feeds into L2 by providing the traversal order that naturally processes nodes left-to-right within levels. Real pattern: queue = [root]; while queue: node = queue.pop(0); process(node); if node.left: queue.append(node.left); if node.right: queue.append(node.right).

examples.trace: Step 1: queue=[1]. Process 1. Add children: queue=[2,3]. Step 2: Process 2. No children. Queue=[3]. Step 3: Process 3. No children. Queue=[]. Order: 1,2,3.

Step 1: queue=[1]. Process 1. Add children: queue=[2,3]. Step 2: Process 2. Add children 4,5: queue=[3,4,5]. Step 3: Process 3. No children. Queue=[4,5]. Step 4: Process 4. No children. Queue=[5]. Step 5: Process 5. No children. Queue=[]. Order: 1,2,3,4,5.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Queue Operations for Tree Traversal
ladders.desc: Enqueue and dequeue nodes in FIFO order
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves using a queue (First-In-First-Out data structure) to manage nodes during tree traversal. The intuition: queues naturally implement level-by-level processing because nodes discovered earlier (at shallower depth) get processed before nodes discovered later (at deeper depth). Key operations: 1) Enqueue (append): add node to back of queue. 2) Dequeue (pop from front): remove and return node from front of queue. For tree traversal: we enqueue child nodes as we discover them, and dequeue nodes to process them. This ensures breadth-first exploration. Algorithm: initialize empty queue; enqueue(root); while queue not empty: node = dequeue(); process(node); enqueue(left_child); enqueue(right_child). Time complexity: O(1) per enqueue/dequeue operation. Space complexity: O(n) for storing nodes in queue. Common mistakes: using list without efficient pop(0) (O(n) time), confusing queue with stack (LIFO), not checking if node exists before enqueuing. This feeds into L3 by providing the mechanism to implement BFS traversal. Real pattern: from collections import deque; queue = deque(); queue.append(root); while queue: node = queue.popleft(); process(node); if node.left: queue.append(node.left); if node.right: queue.append(node.right).

examples.input: Enqueue A, B, C; then dequeue twice
examples.output: Process A then B, queue contains C
examples.trace: Start: queue=[]. Step 1: enqueue(A): queue=[A]. Step 2: enqueue(B): queue=[A,B]. Step 3: enqueue(C): queue=[A,B,C]. Step 4: dequeue(): returns A, queue=[B,C]. Step 5: dequeue(): returns B, queue=[C]. Processed A then B in FIFO order.

examples.output: Queue operations: enqueue(1), dequeue(1), enqueue(2), enqueue(3)
examples.trace: Step 1: enqueue(1): queue=[1]. Step 2: dequeue(): returns 1, queue=[]. Process node 1. Step 3: enqueue left child 2: queue=[2]. Step 4: enqueue right child 3: queue=[2,3]. Complete operations show FIFO behavior.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Node Structure and Children Access
ladders.desc: Understanding tree node representation and accessing left/right children
ladders.explanation: This is the most fundamental concept: how binary trees are represented in code. A binary tree node typically has three components: 1) Value/data stored in node. 2) Reference/pointer to left child node. 3) Reference/pointer to right child node. In many languages, this is implemented as a class/struct with attributes: val, left, right. The intuition: trees are recursive structures where each node can have up to two children. Accessing children is done via node.left and node.right, which may be null/None if child doesn't exist. This is essential for traversal because we need to access children to continue exploring the tree. Basic operations: 1) Create node: Node(val). 2) Check if node exists: node is not None. 3) Access left child: node.left. 4) Access right child: node.right. 5) Check if leaf: node.left is None and node.right is None. Time complexity for access is O(1). Space complexity per node is O(1). Common mistakes: accessing children of null node (null pointer exception), confusing node value with node reference, not understanding that tree is collection of linked nodes. This feeds into L4 by providing the objects we store in the queue. Real pattern: class TreeNode: def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None): self.val = val; self.left = left; self.right = right. To access: if node.left: process(node.left).

examples.input: Node with value 5, left child 3, right child 7
examples.trace: Create node5 = TreeNode(5). Create node3 = TreeNode(3). Create node7 = TreeNode(7). Set node5.left = node3. Set node5.right = node7. Now: node5.val returns 5. node5.left returns reference to node3. node5.left.val returns 3. node5.right returns reference to node7. node5.right.val returns 7. node5.left.left returns None (no grandchild).

Create node10 = TreeNode(10). By default, left and right are None. So: node10.val is 10. node10.left is None. node10.right is None. Checking if leaf: node10.left is None and node10.right is None returns True. Trying to access node10.left.val would cause error because node10.left is None.

testCases.expected: True for node, False for None

approaches.name: Approach 2: Reverse Preorder Depth-First Search
approaches.summary: Use a depth-first search (DFS) with a modified preorder traversal that visits the right child before the left child, and maintain a list of nodes where the first node encountered at each depth is recorded. This leverages the order of traversal to naturally capture right-side views.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(h) where h is the height of the tree (due to recursion stack, O(n) in worst-case for skewed tree)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Right View of Binary Tree using Reverse Preorder DFS
ladders.desc: Complete solution to print right-side visible nodes using modified DFS traversal.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires identifying nodes visible when viewing a binary tree from its right side. The right view consists of the rightmost node at each depth level. Approach 2 uses a depth-first search (DFS) with a modified preorder traversal that visits the right child before the left child. The key insight is that by traversing right-first, the first node encountered at each new depth during DFS will be the rightmost node at that depth from the perspective of the right side. We maintain a list to record these first-encountered nodes per depth. The algorithm recursively traverses the tree, passing the current depth. If the current depth equals the size of the result list (meaning this is the first time reaching this depth), add the node's value to the list. Then recursively call on the right child (incrementing depth), followed by the left child. This ensures right children are processed before left at each level, capturing the rightmost nodes naturally. Time complexity is O(n) as each node is visited once. Space complexity is O(h) for the recursion stack, where h is the tree height (O(n) worst-case for skewed trees). Connection to L1: This full solution integrates the recursive traversal subroutine from L1 with depth tracking and result updating. Common mistakes include not handling empty trees, incorrect depth comparison, or traversing left before right. Real code pattern: Define a recursive helper function that takes node and depth, updates result list if depth == len(result), recurses right then left.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start at root node 1, depth=0, result=[]. Since depth 0 == len(result) 0, add 1 to result: result=[1]. Recurse right to node 3 with depth=1. Step 2: At node 3, depth=1, result=[1]. Since depth 1 == len(result) 1, add 3 to result: result=[1,3]. Recurse right to null (base case). Recurse left to null. Step 3: Back to node 1, recurse left to node 2 with depth=1. At node 2, depth=1, result=[1,3]. Since depth 1 != len(result) 2 (1 < 2), skip adding. Recurse right to node 5 with depth=2. Step 4: At node 5, depth=2, result=[1,3]. Since depth 2 == len(result) 2, add 5 to result: result=[1,3,5]. Recurse right to null, left to null. Step 5: Back to node 2, recurse left to null. Final result: [1,3,5] but note: node 4 is at depth 2 from path 1-3-4? Correction: In tree [1,2,3,null,5,null,4], structure: root=1, left=2 (with right=5), right=3 (with right=4). Trace: Start root 1, add 1. Recurse right to 3, depth=1, add 3. At 3, recurse right to null, left to 4? Wait, 3 has left=null, right=4? Given [1,2,3,null,5,null,4], it's level order: 1 has children 2 and 3; 2 has children null and 5; 3 has children null and 4. So: Step 1: root 1, add 1, recurse right to 3. Step 2: at 3, depth=1, add 3, recurse right to 4. Step 3: at 4, depth=2, add 4, recurse right null, left null. Step 4: back to 3, recurse left null. Step 5: back to 1, recurse left to 2, depth=1, skip adding (depth 1 already has 3). At 2, recurse right to 5, depth=2, skip adding (depth 2 already has 4). Final result: [1,3,4].

Step 1: Root=1, depth=0, result=[], add 1: result=[1]. Recurse right to 3, depth=1. Step 2: At 3, depth=1, result=[1], add 3: result=[1,3]. Recurse right to null, left to null. Step 3: Back to 1, recurse left to 2, depth=1, skip adding. At 2, recurse right to null, left to 4, depth=2. Step 4: At 4, depth=2, result=[1,3], add 4: result=[1,3,4]. Recurse right to null, left to 5, depth=3. Step 5: At 5, depth=3, result=[1,3,4], add 5: result=[1,3,4,5]. Recurse right null, left null. Final result: [1,3,4,5].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Reverse Preorder Traversal with Depth Tracking
ladders.desc: Implement DFS traversal that visits right child before left and tracks depth.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core recursive function for Approach 2. It performs a modified preorder DFS: at each node, it processes the current node, then recursively traverses the right subtree, followed by the left subtree. Additionally, it tracks the current depth in the tree. The function takes a tree node and an integer depth as parameters. The base case is when the node is null, where it returns without doing anything. For non-null nodes, the function will later be integrated with logic to update a result list, but here we focus on the traversal order and depth management. The intuition is that by going right first, we ensure that for any given depth, the rightmost node (from the original tree structure) is encountered before any left nodes at the same depth during the DFS. Depth is incremented by 1 when moving to children. Time complexity for this traversal is O(n) as it visits all nodes. Space complexity is O(h) for recursion stack. Connection to L0: This subroutine provides the traversal framework; L0 adds the result-update condition. Common mistakes: forgetting base case, incorrect depth increment, or reversing left/right order. Real code pattern: Define function dfs(node, depth): if node is null: return; # Process node later; dfs(node.right, depth+1); dfs(node.left, depth+1).

examples.trace: Step 1: Call dfs(1,0). Node not null. Will process 1 later. Step 2: Recurse dfs(1.right=3, depth=0+1=1). Step 3: dfs(3,1): node not null. Process 3 later. Recurse dfs(3.right=null,2): base case return. Recurse dfs(3.left=null,2): return. Back to 3, done. Step 4: Back to 1, recurse dfs(1.left=2, depth=0+1=1). Step 5: dfs(2,1): node not null. Process 2 later. Recurse dfs(2.right=null,2): return. Recurse dfs(2.left=null,2): return. Back to 2, done. Traversal order (processing at each node): 1, 3, 2.

Step 1: dfs(1,0): node not null. Process 1. Recurse dfs(1.right=2,1). Step 2: dfs(2,1): node not null. Process 2. Recurse dfs(2.right=3,2). Step 3: dfs(3,2): node not null. Process 3. Recurse dfs(3.right=null,3): return. Recurse dfs(3.left=null,3): return. Back to 3, done. Step 4: Back to 2, recurse dfs(2.left=null,2): return. Back to 2, done. Step 5: Back to 1, recurse dfs(1.left=null,1): return. Traversal order: 1, 2, 3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — First Node per Depth in Right-First Traversal Captures Right View
ladders.desc: Insight that rightmost nodes are first encountered when traversing right before left.
ladders.explanation: This core logic explains why the modified DFS works for the right view problem. In a binary tree, the right view requires the rightmost node at each depth level. During a DFS, if we always visit the right child before the left child at every node, then for any given depth, the first node we encounter at that depth during the traversal will be the rightmost node at that depth. This happens because the traversal prioritizes right paths, so it reaches the rightmost node in a level before exploring leftward nodes at the same depth. By maintaining a list where we record the first node value per depth (e.g., when depth equals the current size of the list), we naturally collect the right view. The intuition leverages the order of visitation: depth-first with right priority ensures that at each new depth reached, the node visited is the rightmost one visible from the right side. This avoids needing level-order traversal or explicit comparisons. Time and space are as per DFS. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in L1's traversal by adding a condition to update results. Common mistakes: misunderstanding traversal order, not resetting depth correctly, or incorrectly identifying 'first encounter'. Real code pattern: In dfs, if depth == len(result): result.append(node.val).

examples.input: Tree: [1,2,3], show first encounter per depth
examples.trace: Perform right-first DFS: Start at root 1 depth 0. First time at depth 0, record 1. Move right to 3 depth 1. First time at depth 1, record 3. Move left to 2 depth 1, but depth 1 already recorded, skip. So first encounters: depth 0: 1, depth 1: 3.

examples.input: Tree: [1,2,3,4,5], show first encounter per depth
examples.trace: Right-first DFS: Node 1 depth 0 first, record 1. Right to 3 depth 1 first, record 3. Right from 3 is null, left from 3 is null. Back to 1, left to 2 depth 1, depth 1 already recorded, skip. From 2, right to 5 depth 2 first, record 5. Left to 4 depth 2, already recorded, skip. First encounters: 1,3,5.

ladders.title: Building Block — Depth Tracking in Tree Traversal
ladders.desc: Maintain and update depth parameter during recursive tree traversal.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves tracking the depth (or level) of nodes during a tree traversal. Depth is defined as the distance from the root, with root at depth 0. In recursive traversals like DFS, depth is passed as a parameter to the recursive function. Each time we move to a child node (left or right), we increment the depth by 1. This allows us to know at what level each node is located during traversal. The intuition is that tree algorithms often need to perform operations based on depth, such as collecting nodes per level or identifying nodes at specific depths. By propagating depth through recursion, we avoid global variables and keep the traversal stateless. Time and space are inherent to the traversal. Connection to L2: Depth tracking enables the core logic of recording first node per depth. Common mistakes: forgetting to increment depth, using global depth incorrectly, or mismatching depth with array indices. Real code pattern: In function dfs(node, depth): if node is null: return; # Use depth; dfs(node.right, depth+1); dfs(node.left, depth+1).

examples.output: Node 1 depth 0, Node 3 depth 1, Node 2 depth 1
examples.trace: Start dfs(1,0): depth=0 for node 1. Recurse dfs(3,0+1=1): depth=1 for node 3. Recurse dfs(null,2) and dfs(null,2) return. Back to 1, recurse dfs(2,0+1=1): depth=1 for node 2. Depths: 1:0, 3:1, 2:1.

examples.output: Node 1 depth 0, Node 2 depth 1, Node 3 depth 2
examples.trace: dfs(1,0): depth 0 for 1. dfs(2,1): depth 1 for 2. dfs(3,2): depth 2 for 3. Depths recorded.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Recursive Tree Traversal (DFS)
ladders.desc: Visit all nodes in a binary tree using depth-first search recursion.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves traversing a binary tree to visit each node exactly once using a depth-first search (DFS) approach. DFS explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. For binary trees, common DFS orders include preorder (node, left, right), inorder (left, node, right), and postorder (left, right, node). Here, we focus on preorder as a foundation. The algorithm is recursive: define a function that takes a tree node. If the node is null, return (base case). Otherwise, process the current node (e.g., print its value), then recursively call the function on the left child, followed by the right child. The intuition is that recursion naturally mirrors the tree structure, with each call handling a subtree. Time complexity is O(n) for n nodes. Space complexity is O(h) due to recursion stack. Connection to L3: This traversal is extended in L3 by adding a depth parameter. Common mistakes: missing base case, infinite recursion, or incorrect order of left/right calls. Real code pattern: def dfs(node): if node is None: return; # Process node; dfs(node.left); dfs(node.right).

examples.trace: Start dfs(1): node not null. Process 1. Recurse dfs(2): process 2. Recurse dfs(2.left=null): return. Recurse dfs(2.right=null): return. Back to 1, recurse dfs(3): process 3. Recurse dfs(3.left=null): return. Recurse dfs(3.right=null): return. Order: 1,2,3.
dfs(1): process 1. dfs(1.left=null): return. dfs(1.right=2): process 2. dfs(2.left=3): process 3. dfs(3.left=null): return. dfs(3.right=null): return. dfs(2.right=null): return. Order: 1,2,3.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Node Structure and Recursion Base Case
ladders.desc: Understand binary tree node representation and basic recursive function with base case.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers the basic building blocks for tree algorithms. A binary tree is a hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children: left and right. In code, a node is typically represented as an object with attributes: value (or val), left (pointer to left child), and right (pointer to right child). The tree can be empty, represented by a null (or None) root. Recursion is a key technique for tree problems, where a function calls itself to solve subproblems (subtrees). The base case is crucial to prevent infinite recursion: for trees, the base case is when the node is null, meaning we've reached an empty subtree. The intuition is that trees are naturally recursive—each node roots a subtree, so algorithms often define operations in terms of the current node and its children. Time and space are not analyzed at this basic level. Connection to L4: This foundation enables the recursive traversal in L4. Common mistakes: not handling null nodes, assuming non-empty trees, or incorrect node structure. Real code pattern: class TreeNode: def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None): self.val = val; self.left = left; self.right = right. For recursion: def f(node): if node is None: return; # Recursive calls on node.left and node.right.

examples.trace: Create root node with val=1. Set left child as new node with val=2, left=None, right=None. Set right child as new node with val=3, left=None, right=None. Structure: root.val=1, root.left.val=2, root.right.val=3.
The tree has no nodes, so root is None. In code, this is represented as a null pointer.

testCases.expected: TreeNode(1, TreeNode(2, TreeNode(4), TreeNode(5)), TreeNode(3, TreeNode(6), TreeNode(7)))

approaches.name: Approach 3: Iterative DFS with Depth Tracking
approaches.summary: Perform an iterative DFS using a stack, where each element tracks both the node and its current depth. Update a result list only when encountering a new depth for the first time during a right-first traversal, simulating the reverse preorder approach without recursion.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(h) where h is the height of the tree (stack space, O(n) in worst-case for skewed tree)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Right View of Binary Tree using Iterative DFS with Depth Tracking
ladders.desc: Complete solution implementing iterative DFS with stack to track nodes and depths, updating result when encountering new depth first time in right-first traversal.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the right view problem by performing an iterative depth-first search (DFS) using a stack, where each stack element stores both a tree node and its current depth. The key insight is that when traversing in a right-first manner (visiting right child before left child), the first node encountered at each depth will be the rightmost visible node from that level when viewed from the right side. We maintain a result list that gets updated only when we encounter a new depth for the first time during traversal. The algorithm starts by pushing the root node with depth 0 onto the stack. While the stack is not empty, we pop the top element, check if its depth equals the current size of the result list (meaning we haven't seen this depth before), and if so, add the node's value to result. Then we push the left and right children onto the stack with incremented depth, but crucially push the left child first and then the right child. This ordering ensures that when we pop from the stack (LIFO), we process the right child before the left child, simulating a right-first DFS. The time complexity is O(n) as we visit each node exactly once. Space complexity is O(h) where h is the height of the tree due to stack storage, which becomes O(n) in worst-case for a skewed tree. This approach avoids recursion overhead while maintaining the logical structure of DFS traversal with explicit depth tracking.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize stack = [(1,0)], result = []
Step 2: Pop (1,0), depth=0, result size=0, add 1 → result=[1]
Step 3: Push left child (2,1), push right child (3,1) → stack=[(2,1),(3,1)]
Step 4: Pop (3,1), depth=1, result size=1, add 3 → result=[1,3]
Step 5: Push left child (null), push right child (4,2) → stack=[(2,1),(4,2)]
Step 6: Pop (4,2), depth=2, result size=2, add 4 → result=[1,3,4]
Step 7: Pop (2,1), depth=1, result size=3 (skip, already have depth 1)
Step 8: Push left child (5,2), push right child (null) → stack=[(5,2)]
Step 9: Pop (5,2), depth=2, result size=3 (skip, already have depth 2)
Step 10: Stack empty, return [1,3,4]

Step 1: Initialize stack = [(1,0)], result = []
Step 2: Pop (1,0), depth=0, result size=0, add 1 → result=[1]
Step 3: Push left child (2,1), push right child (3,1) → stack=[(2,1),(3,1)]
Step 4: Pop (3,1), depth=1, result size=1, add 3 → result=[1,3]
Step 5: Push left child (null), push right child (null) → stack=[(2,1)]
Step 6: Pop (2,1), depth=1, result size=2 (skip, already have depth 1)
Step 7: Push left child (4,2), push right child (null) → stack=[(4,2)]
Step 8: Pop (4,2), depth=2, result size=2, add 4 → result=[1,3,4]
Step 9: Push left child (5,3), push right child (null) → stack=[(5,3)]
Step 10: Pop (5,3), depth=3, result size=3, add 5 → result=[1,3,4,5]
Step 11: Stack empty, return [1,3,4,5]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Right-First Iterative DFS Traversal with Depth Tracking
ladders.desc: Implement iterative DFS using stack that processes nodes in right-first order while tracking depth of each node.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine forms the core traversal mechanism for the right view problem. The algorithm performs an iterative depth-first search using a stack data structure, where each stack element is a tuple containing a tree node and its current depth. The traversal follows a right-first approach: when pushing children onto the stack, we push the left child first and then the right child. Due to the LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) nature of stacks, this means the right child gets processed before the left child when we pop elements. This right-first ordering is crucial because when viewing a binary tree from the right side, the rightmost node at each depth is what's visible. By tracking depth alongside each node, we can identify when we first encounter a particular depth level during traversal. The algorithm starts by pushing the root node with depth 0. While the stack is not empty, we pop the top element, process the node (in the full problem, this means checking if we should add it to the result), then push its children with depth+1. The time complexity is O(n) as each node is pushed and popped exactly once. Space complexity is O(h) where h is tree height, representing the maximum stack size during traversal. This sub-routine provides the traversal framework that the full solution builds upon by adding logic to capture only the first node at each depth.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize stack = [(1,0)]
Step 2: Pop (1,0) → process node 1 at depth 0
Step 3: Push left child (2,1), then right child (3,1) → stack=[(2,1),(3,1)]
Step 4: Pop (3,1) → process node 3 at depth 1
Step 5: Push left child (null), right child (null) → stack=[(2,1)]
Step 6: Pop (2,1) → process node 2 at depth 1
Step 7: Stack empty, traversal complete

Step 1: Initialize stack = [(1,0)]
Step 2: Pop (1,0) → process node 1 at depth 0
Step 3: Push left child (2,1), then right child (3,1) → stack=[(2,1),(3,1)]
Step 4: Pop (3,1) → process node 3 at depth 1
Step 5: Push left child (null), right child (null) → stack=[(2,1)]
Step 6: Pop (2,1) → process node 2 at depth 1
Step 7: Push left child (4,2), then right child (5,2) → stack=[(4,2),(5,2)]
Step 8: Pop (5,2) → process node 5 at depth 2
Step 9: Pop (4,2) → process node 4 at depth 2
Step 10: Stack empty, traversal complete

ladders.title: Core Logic — First Node Capture at Each Depth During Right-First Traversal
ladders.desc: The insight that during right-first DFS, the first node encountered at each depth is the rightmost visible node.
ladders.explanation: This core logic represents the fundamental insight that makes the right view algorithm work. When performing a depth-first search in a right-first manner (processing right subtree before left subtree), the first time we reach any given depth level during traversal, we are guaranteed to be looking at the rightmost node at that depth. This happens because our traversal prioritizes the right side of the tree at every decision point. Imagine you're standing to the right of a binary tree: at each level, the node farthest to the right blocks your view of any nodes to its left at the same level. During right-first DFS, we naturally encounter these rightmost nodes first as we go deeper into the tree. The algorithm leverages this by maintaining a result list and only adding a node's value when we encounter a depth we haven't seen before. We can track this by comparing the current node's depth with the size of the result list: if depth equals result size, it means this is the first time we're seeing this depth level. This elegant relationship works because result size starts at 0 (depth 0), and each time we add a node, result size increments by 1, perfectly matching the next depth we might encounter. This logic transforms a simple traversal into a right view solution without needing level-order traversal or complex bookkeeping.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize result = []
Step 2: Process node 1 at depth 0: depth(0) == result size(0) → add 1 → result=[1]
Step 3: Process node 3 at depth 1: depth(1) == result size(1) → add 3 → result=[1,3]
Step 4: Process node 2 at depth 1: depth(1) != result size(2) → skip
Step 5: Process node 4 at depth 2: depth(2) == result size(2) → add 4 → result=[1,3,4]

examples.input: Right-first traversal: nodes at depths [A(depth0), C(depth1), E(depth2), D(depth2), B(depth1), F(depth2)]
examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize result = []
Step 2: Process A at depth 0: depth(0) == result size(0) → add A → result=[A]
Step 3: Process C at depth 1: depth(1) == result size(1) → add C → result=[A,C]
Step 4: Process E at depth 2: depth(2) == result size(2) → add E → result=[A,C,E]
Step 5: Process D at depth 2: depth(2) != result size(3) → skip
Step 6: Process B at depth 1: depth(1) != result size(3) → skip
Step 7: Process F at depth 2: depth(2) != result size(3) → skip
Final result: [A,C,E]

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterative Tree Traversal Using Stack with State Tracking
ladders.desc: Implement iterative DFS on binary tree using stack while maintaining additional state information (depth) for each node.
ladders.explanation: This building block teaches how to perform iterative depth-first traversal of a binary tree using an explicit stack, while carrying additional state information (in this case, depth) alongside each node. Unlike recursive DFS which uses the call stack implicitly, iterative DFS uses an explicit stack data structure that we manage manually. Each element pushed onto the stack contains both the tree node to process and its associated depth in the tree. The algorithm begins by pushing the root node with depth 0. We then enter a loop that continues while the stack is not empty. In each iteration, we pop the top element from the stack, which gives us both the current node and its depth. After processing the node (which in the full solution means checking if we should add it to the result), we need to push its children onto the stack for future processing. Here's the key: we push the left child first, then the right child, each with depth incremented by 1. Because stacks are LIFO (Last-In-First-Out), this means the right child will be popped before the left child, creating our desired right-first traversal order. This approach allows us to traverse the tree without recursion while maintaining the depth context we need. The time complexity is O(n) as we process each node once, and space complexity is O(h) where h is tree height, representing the maximum stack depth.

examples.output: Stack operations showing nodes with depths
examples.trace:
Step 1: Push (1,0) → stack=[(1,0)]
Step 2: Pop → get (1,0), depth=0
Step 3: Push children: push (2,1), then push (3,1) → stack=[(2,1),(3,1)]
Step 4: Pop → get (3,1), depth=1
Step 5: Push children: (null skipped), (null skipped) → stack=[(2,1)]
Step 6: Pop → get (2,1), depth=1
Step 7: Push children: (null skipped), (null skipped) → stack=[]
Step 8: Stack empty, done

examples.output: Stack operations showing nodes with depths
examples.trace:
Step 1: Push (1,0) → stack=[(1,0)]
Step 2: Pop → get (1,0), depth=0
Step 3: Push children: push (2,1), then push (3,1) → stack=[(2,1),(3,1)]
Step 4: Pop → get (3,1), depth=1
Step 5: Push children: (null skipped), (null skipped) → stack=[(2,1)]
Step 6: Pop → get (2,1), depth=1
Step 7: Push children: push (4,2), then push (null) → stack=[(4,2)]
Step 8: Pop → get (4,2), depth=2
Step 9: Push children: (null skipped), (null skipped) → stack=[]
Step 10: Stack empty, done

testCases.expected: Stack operations: push(1,0), pop(1,0), push(2,1), pop(2,1)
Stack operations: push(1,0), pop(1,0), push(3,1), pop(3,1)
Stack operations: push(1,0), pop(1,0), push(2,1), push(3,1), pop(3,1), pop(2,1)
Stack operations: push(1,0), pop(1,0), push(2,1), push(3,1), pop(3,1), pop(2,1), push(4,2), pop(4,2)
Stack operations: push(1,0), pop(1,0), push(2,1), push(3,1), pop(3,1), pop(2,1), push(5,2), pop(5,2)
Stack operations: push(1,0), pop(1,0), push(2,1), push(3,1), pop(3,1), pop(2,1), push(4,2), push(5,2), pop(5,2), pop(4,2)
Stack operations: push(1,0), pop(1,0), push(2,1), push(3,1), pop(3,1), push(6,2), pop(6,2), pop(2,1), push(4,2), push(5,2), pop(5,2), pop(4,2)
Stack operations: push(1,0), pop(1,0), push(2,1), push(3,1), pop(3,1), push(6,2), push(7,2), pop(7,2), pop(6,2), pop(2,1), push(4,2), push(5,2), pop(5,2), pop(4,2)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Stack Manipulation for Tree Node Processing
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations of pushing tree nodes onto stack and popping them for processing in LIFO order.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation covers the fundamental stack manipulations needed for iterative tree traversal. A stack is a LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) data structure that supports two primary operations: push (add to top) and pop (remove from top). For tree traversal, we use the stack to keep track of nodes we need to visit. The algorithm follows this pattern: 1) Start by pushing the root node onto the stack. 2) While the stack is not empty, pop the top node from the stack. 3) Process the popped node (which in our full solution means checking its depth and potentially adding it to result). 4) Push the node's children onto the stack for future processing. The order in which we push children matters significantly: if we push left child first then right child, due to LIFO behavior, the right child will be popped before the left child, creating a right-first traversal. This is the reverse of what we might intuitively expect, which is why understanding stack behavior is crucial. We must also handle null nodes gracefully by not pushing them onto the stack. Each push operation stores both the node reference and its depth as a tuple. The pop operation retrieves both pieces of information. This basic stack manipulation forms the engine that drives our iterative DFS traversal without recursion.

examples.input: Process node A with children B (left) and C (right), depth tracking
examples.trace:
Step 1: Start with stack containing [(A,0)]
Step 2: Pop → get A with depth 0
Step 3: Process node A
Step 4: Push left child B with depth 1 → stack=[(B,1)]
Step 5: Push right child C with depth 1 → stack=[(B,1),(C,1)]
Step 6: Next iteration will pop C first (right child), then B (left child)

examples.input: Process node X with only left child Y, depth 2
examples.trace:
Step 1: Start with stack containing [(X,2)]
Step 2: Pop → get X with depth 2
Step 3: Process node X
Step 4: Push left child Y with depth 3 → stack=[(Y,3)]
Step 5: Right child is null, so don't push anything
Step 6: Next iteration will pop Y

testCases.input: Stack: [(1,0)], process: pop, push children (2,1) and (3,1)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Node Structure and Depth Concept
ladders.desc: Understanding binary tree node structure with left/right pointers and the concept of depth/level in trees.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers the basic building blocks needed to understand tree algorithms. A binary tree node typically has three components: a value (the data stored in the node), a left child pointer (reference to left subtree), and a right child pointer (reference to right subtree). In many implementations, nodes also have a reference to their parent, though for this problem we only need child pointers. The depth of a node (also called level) is its distance from the root node. The root has depth 0. Its immediate children have depth 1. Their children have depth 2, and so on. Depth increases by 1 each time we move from a parent to its child. We can think of depth as the number of edges from the root to the node. In a binary tree, each node can have at most two children: left and right. Some nodes may have only one child or no children (leaf nodes). Understanding this hierarchical structure is essential because tree algorithms often involve traversing from parent to children recursively or iteratively. The depth concept becomes crucial for problems like right view where we need to identify nodes at each depth level. This foundation assumes no prior knowledge beyond basic programming concepts.

examples.input: Simple tree: root A with left child B and right child C
examples.trace:
Node A: value=A, left=B, right=C, depth=0
Node B: value=B, left=null, right=null, depth=1 (child of A)
Node C: value=C, left=null, right=null, depth=1 (child of A)
Tree structure: A(depth0) has children B(depth1) and C(depth1)

examples.input: Tree: root X with left child Y, Y has right child Z
examples.trace:
Node X: value=X, left=Y, right=null, depth=0
Node Y: value=Y, left=null, right=Z, depth=1 (child of X)
Node Z: value=Z, left=null, right=null, depth=2 (child of Y)
Tree structure: X(depth0) → Y(depth1) → Z(depth2)

testCases.input: Root(100), left(200), left's left(300), left's right(400)
Root(A), right(B), B's left(C), B's right(D)
